data.title: Keep Calm And Keep Buying These Dividends
data.description:
Staying the course as a dividend investor can be very challenging at times, and thus far, 2025 has illustrated this as well as any year in recent memory. Over the past several years, dividend growth investors, especially those in funds like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), have gotten left behind the S&P 500 (SPY) with some very lackluster performance, as the market's sentiment has been overwhelmingly favorably directed towards mega-cap tech stocks, rapidly growing AI companies like Palantir (PLTR), and of course, Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Further compounding the headwinds for dividend stocks have been the higher interest rates plaguing the sector, and in particular REITs (VNQ) and other high-yielding sub-sectors of the dividend space.

As such, it can be easy for investors to throw in the towel and instead chase whatever stocks are hot right now. Moreover, earlier this year, the market crashed in a panic over the rollout of the Trump administration's tariffs on virtually all the U.S.'s trading partners across the world. Many observers forecast a severe recession, and as a result, stocks sold off very aggressively.

As a dividend investor, it would have been easy to panic there, sell your stocks, and try to guard against further losses, because it seemed like the market was headed lower day after day. However, many who did that ended up getting left behind as the market quickly rebounded in the days and weeks that followed. Therefore, if you remained calm and bought the dip, you would be better off today than you were before the crash even occurred. With this in view, in today's article, I am going to share why I think investors should continue to keep calm and stay the course with dividend investing, even though they have suffered from significant underperformance in recent years. In addition, I am going to share some of the best dividend stocks to buy today for long-term income and total returns on a risk-adjusted basis, even as investors wait to see if the Fed will finally give dividend investors much-needed interest rate relief.

Why Dividend Investors Should Stay The Course

Let us address each of these issues in turn and explain why investors who stay the course are highly likely to end up ahead over the long term. First of all, the concerns about the AI boom are likely short-lived because eventually the benefits of AI are going to begin to flow through to dividend stocks. In many cases, they already are, as we see dividend growth machines like Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC), Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) (BIPC), Enbridge (ENB), and many others announcing major growth opportunities coming from the data center build-out and the need for increased energy production and infrastructure to support the AI boom. This will likely only become more evident in the coming quarters and years, which should drive strong growth in these and similar dividend stocks.

Additionally, even industrials and consumer stocks like FedEx (FDX), PepsiCo (PEP), McDonald's (MCD), Walmart (WMT), and many others will likely be huge beneficiaries of the AI boom, as advanced models get employed and their large treasure troves of proprietary data as well as economies of scale can be deployed to leverage their competitive strengths over peers. Additionally, dividend-paying biotech and pharma giants like Pfizer (PFE) will likely benefit as well, as their R&D will likely become much more efficient once advanced AI models become much more prominent in the drug development process.

Meanwhile, the concerns about the interest rate headwinds are legitimate, and certainly, there is a viable scenario in which they last for an extended period to come, especially on the long end of the curve. However, ultimately, it should not matter too much to dividend investors deploying capital today. This is because the higher interest rates are largely already priced in and, in some cases, I think, are priced in too much. Therefore, you are able to buy dividend stocks at higher yields and lower valuations than you would be able to otherwise. Moreover, as long as you are insisting on quality companies with strong balance sheets, which you should be doing anyway as a dividend investor, higher for longer interest rates should not be that big of a deal. If anything, they should be cheered because they allow you to grow your passive income stream faster than you would otherwise have been. Meanwhile, if interest rates do move down in the coming years, it could provide a very powerful catalyst for share price appreciation and therefore, elevated total returns on capital deployed today.

Finally, concerns about an economic downturn, whether they are caused by tariffs and trade uncertainty or otherwise, should not be overly feared by dividend investors either. This is especially true if you employ sound principles of dividend investing, which include adequate diversification across sectors and insisting on putting the bulk of your capital to work in durable, defensive business models with strong balance sheets and well-covered growing dividends. Therefore, in the event of an economic downturn, your dividend income stream should remain quite stable and likely even continue to grow, while in turn giving you more attractive prices to be able to redeploy the capital into more attractive opportunities. Additionally, an economic downturn would likely solve the interest rate problem by forcing the Fed to cut rates and bring down interest rates overall, thereby helping to offset economic headwinds on stock prices. And even if the stock prices do not immediately rebound from a fall in interest rates due to economic concerns overshadowing them, it should lower the cost of capital for the businesses that you hold in your portfolio, thereby enabling them to accelerate growth investments, which should bear fruit over time, and eventually the stock prices will catch up with that fact.

Dividends To Buy

With all of that said, what are some of the best dividend stocks to buy today? A great place to start is with Realty Income (O), as it is widely considered the gold standard of real estate investment trusts with a long history of over a quarter century of growing its dividend every year at a mid-single-digit CAGR, thereby making it an investment that should outpace inflation over the long term. In addition, it offers a current dividend yield of nearly 6%, which is well covered by cash flows, and those cash flows are extremely durable given that they come from a portfolio of thousands of triple-net lease properties that are leased to high-quality tenants, many of which are investment grade and are also largely resistant to recessions and e-commerce headwinds.

With an A- credit rating underpinning the business, O stands out as a company that should withstand technological disruption, economic turmoil, and higher for longer interest rates. Moreover, as a bond proxy, it should enjoy significant upside if interest rates do falter. In the meantime, investors can collect a juicy dividend yield and continue to reinvest it in buying shares at an attractive price while waiting for the interest rate environment to change.

Another great opportunity to buy right now is Oneok (OKE), as it is a well-diversified energy infrastructure company that stands to benefit from numerous potential catalysts, including growing energy demand for the AI boom as well as energy export tailwinds. Meanwhile, it issues a 1099 tax form that makes it a C-corporation, and yet it trades at a compelling discount to many of its C-corp peers like Kinder Morgan (KMI) and the Williams Companies (WMB) on an EV/EBITDA basis, while offering a dividend yield of over 5%, attractive growth potential, and trading at a much lower enterprise value to EBITDA multiple.

A third attractive dividend investment is to simply buy the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF ((SCHD), as it has one of the lowest expense ratios of all dividend growth ETFs at 0.06%, is diversified across over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks, offers a dividend yield of nearly 4% on a forward-looking basis, and has a dividend track record of growing its payout at a near 11% CAGR over the past decade.

Last but not least, alternative asset managers like Blackstone (BX), Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) (BN), and Blue Owl Capital (OWL) are all highly compelling dividend investment opportunities right now, as they offer dividend yields in the 3–5% range on a forward-looking basis, double-digit expected annualized growth rates for both their underlying cash flows and dividend payouts, and have strong investment-grade credit ratings. Meanwhile, a sharp decline in interest rates should also be a huge boon to their businesses since they tend to employ significant leverage in their underlying holdings and are also viewed as competing products with traditional investments like bonds.

Investor Takeaway

Staying the course with dividend investing has not been easy recently due to the AI stock market boom largely bypassing the sector, interest rates remaining higher for longer than many were hoping, and concerns about how trade tensions and other macro risks may affect dividend-paying stocks. However, staying the course is important to keep the compounding process going, leading to large increases in passive income from dividends over the long term. A quick look at SCHD's exemplary long-term dividend growth—despite also paying out a significantly higher dividend yield than SPY does—tells a powerful story of the power of compounding with dividends:

While stock prices rise and fall with the whims of Mr. Market, the hard power of dividends is what matters most to me in the long term. Therefore, I continue to buy blue-chip dividend growth stocks whenever they go on sale. In fact, I view the recent underperformance in the dividend stock sector as a huge gift from Mr. Market because it has given me an opportunity to lock in attractive yields and valuations on these stocks. As a result, I continue to opportunistically yet aggressively deploy capital at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Conagra Brands: Poor Operating Results But Steeply Discounted Valuation
data.description:
I upgraded shares of Conagra Brands (NYSE:CAG) to a buy rating back in February.

While the company was clearly facing numerous challenges, my view was that its valuation had dipped far enough that it was hard to deny the upside. So far, that call has left a bad taste in my mouth. Shares have slid another 20% since my prior article and are now off by nearly half over the past five years:

Was I totally wrong here? Or has the market become overly pessimistic about this packaged food firm?

Q4 Results: Unsatisfactory

To start off, Conagra's fiscal year Q4 results, released earlier this month, were a disappointment. Though Conagra's recent earnings reports haven't been anything special, this one was an underwhelming one even against that standard.

Conagra missed on both earnings and revenues. The 56 cents of EPS fell five cents short of expectations. Revenues of $2.78 billion were missed by $100 million, resulting in a 4.3% year-over-year decline. In the current inflationary environment with significant input cost increases, not being able to maintain even flat sales is a worrisome sign indeed.

There was no silver lining to be found in the company's 2026 guidance, either. Conagra guided to adjusted EPS of between $1.70 and $1.85, falling well short of the prior $2.46 analyst consensus. Furthermore, the company guided organic sales growth of between -1 and +1 percent for the upcoming year. If investors had been wishing that 2026 was going to be the year that Conagra finally snapped out of its earnings slump, those hopes now appear to be dashed.

To explain the weak results, Conagra used justifications that we've heard frequently in the consumer staples space over the past year. Namely, there's been inflation in the supply chain, especially so for proteins. Meanwhile, lackluster consumer demand has made it tough to push through price increases at a pace adequate to offset the rising input costs. This results in margin pressure and falling earnings per share.

Unfortunately, there's little sign of this adverse trend reversing course. Key inputs such as beef prices continue to rise, which indicates that, at least in the first part of Conagra's fiscal year 2026, we will likely see more inflationary pressures on its operations.

The company is doing what it can in terms of trying to cut costs, manage efficiencies, and so on to reduce the blow to margins.

But, as I've written previously, I don't think Conagra's brands are particularly strong, especially among younger consumers. With a few exceptions, Conagra's products aren't something like Hershey's (HSY) chocolate, which can demand a big premium over the store brand. The sort of attachment people have to their favorite flavor of soda or chocolate, for example, doesn't necessarily apply to Conagra's vegetable products or frozen dinners. As a result, Conagra risks losing share to store brands if it tries to raise prices too quickly.

Additionally, we have a new headwind here. The new U.S. government budget limits SNAP benefits (formerly known as food stamps) eligibility. From my understanding, Conagra has more exposure to government-assisted grocery channels than many of its peers. I don't have a sense of how to quantify how much the hit may be here until we see the actual impacts of the changes in the programs and the resulting impact on consumer behavior. I wouldn't be surprised, however, if Conagra is proportionally one of the larger losers in its peer group as a result.

Is The 7% Dividend Yield Safe?

With the significant drop in the company's earnings guidance, we now have to start asking questions about the dividend's sustainability. Based on both the concluded fiscal year 2025 earnings and the projection for 2026, Conagra can still cover its dividend out of earnings for the time being.

However, the gap between the current FY '26 estimate of $1.75 and Conagra's $1.40 dividend payout is not that large. Keep in mind, additionally, that all 13 of the latest analyst revisions have been to the downside. We could see both the $1.75 number for 2026 and $1.97 number for FY'27 continue to drop, especially if commodity prices keep rising, thus further pressuring margins. In fact, I would watch the 2027 EPS figure fairly closely, as I'm skeptical that the company will post 12% EPS growth on flat revenues. I simply don't see that sort of latent pricing power at Conagra right now, so I'd be on watch for another trim to EPS guidance.

If EPS stays sub-$2/share for the next few years, should management keep paying out the current $1.40 per share dividend?

That depends on its priorities. The company is also wanting to pay down debt and invest in its supply chain in order to reduce costs and improve its long-term competitiveness. At some point, you'd have to think that these investments in the business and in shoring up the balance sheet might start to come before the dividend if the current EPS slump persists. Clearly, the large dividend is not doing much to support the share price at this point.

I wouldn't say management needs to cut the dividend at this point, and it could easily be sustained for a good while at today's rate. But I wouldn't be at all surprised if we do see a cut over the next 12 to 18 months, either.

Valuation: The Main Reason For Optimism

I've been talking at length about the negatives around Conagra. Yet, I am sitting here with a buy rating on CAG stock today. Why is that?

I want to emphasize this is a speculative situation. You could certainly lose money on Conagra from this price if things don't go well. But for risk-tolerant investors, I see a real upside case here.

For one thing, I believe we will see more shareholder activism and more takeover offers in the space. We already saw private food giant Mars announce its deal to take over cereal company Kellanova (K), with that deal receiving more regulatory approvals and seeming likely to close fairly soon. Following up on that, earlier in July, Ferrero Group announced that it will be acquiring WK Kellogg (KLG) at a large premium to its prior trading price. These two deals combined have given some life to the broader U.S. packaged food space and seemingly put a floor under some of these stocks, as there is now the idea that acquirers will be kicking the tires if valuations keep dropping.

Furthermore, it appears that KraftHeinz (KHC) could be willing to split itself up in an effort to unlock shareholder value and help lift its sagging stock price.

While consumer staples have had a dreadful past five years, the category has long been a favorite of defensive investors that valued steady and consistent cash flows. I wouldn't be surprised if we see more M&A across the space to take advantage of current depressed valuation multiples.

Specifically, I could see a larger food company or private equity shop taking a swing at Conagra if the valuation continues to get even cheaper. Also, consider that Conagra spun off its potato business, Lamb Weston (LW), in a transaction that was quite beneficial. LW stock initially soared once it split off from Conagra. Given that history, I wouldn't be surprised if Conagra again turns to a spin-off or asset sale to try to improve its valuation picture.

There's also room for the staples sector to revalue more broadly when the macroeconomic cycle changes. At some point, I expect an economic slowdown and an ensuing decline in interest rates. This should lead investors to assign more value to stable, cash flow-generative, recession-resistant businesses such as Conagra.

Finally, this gets us to valuation. Conagra is trading at its lowest levels on an EV/EBITDA and EV/Free Cash Flow basis over the past decade:

Meanwhile, on an earnings basis, Conagra is selling around 11x projected FY'26 earnings. That's a roughly 9% earnings yield. Right now, most of that is being used to fund the 7.3% dividend yield. If Conagra maintains that dividend yield, shareholders should do fairly well overall, with even a modest increase in the share price going forward.

If Conagra cuts the dividend, the 9% earnings yield will still be there and could be used to pay down debt quickly. Conagra currently has a BBB- credit rating according to Fitch, and about 25% of the company's operating income in any given year goes to interest service. If Conagra does reduce the dividend and channel those funds into paying down debt, I'd see that as an acceptable outcome for shareholders as well.

I doubt Conagra and other packaged food companies will go back to their pre-2020 valuation ratios. Conagra in particular simply doesn't have particularly strong brands, so I would not be banking on a return to a low-to-mid 20s P/E ratio anytime soon.

That said, Conagra is arguably underperforming a bit now. The pressure in commodity prices, in particular, is likely to subside at some point and allow Conagra to see some lift to profit margins. Combine that with a move up to, say, a 14-16x P/E ratio, and we could see CAG shares return to the $30-$35 range over the next few years.

And if the company doesn't deliver positive returns for shareholders soon, I see a decent shot at the company being an activist investor target or having a takeover offer come in. All this leads me to stick with a buy rating despite Conagra's continuing string of disappointing earnings results.

data.title: Alector: Struggles Likely To Continue For Brain Science Company
data.description:
This is my third Alector (ALEC) article, following 07/2024's "Alector: Brain Science Play With High Expenses And Few Catalysts". In Few Catalysts, I rated Alector a hold. It was trading at $5.55 at the time. It has since dropped ~70%. Ouch.

In this article, slightly woozy from the huge beating following Few Catalysts. I am daring to step into the ring again. Today's contest is to determine if Alector's huge beatdown leaves it as a bargain we should scoop up or a falling knife we should avoid like the plague.

In this article, I will reference Alector's 02/26/2025, Q4/2024 10-K its 05/08/2025, Q1/2025 10-Q and its 06/2025 website, Corporate Presentation.

Alector Brain Carrier [ABC] technology for blood-brain barrier [BBB] transport is its special sauce.

In the introductory section to its 10-K Alector sets itself the bold mission of rendering degenerative brain disorders history. It lays claim to special understanding of:

...the complex mechanisms that drive neurodegenerative diseases, including the roles of toxic misfolded proteins, deficient proteins, and lysosomal, immune system, and neuronal dysfunction.

It goes on to explain that its:

... programs leverage ... deep understanding of the genetic underpinnings of ...[brain] diseases, combined with ... expertise in drug development, proprietary protein engineering, antibody discovery, and ... innovative Alector Brain Carrier [ABC] technology for blood-brain barrier [BBB] transport. ... ABC aims to enhance the delivery of therapeutics, achieve deeper brain penetration and efficacy at lower doses, and ultimately improve patient outcomes while reducing costs.

Presentation slides 7-9 provide more granular descriptions:

Slide 7 - Alector Brain Carrier [ABC]: Designed for lower dosing, improved efficacy and safety, and subcu delivery across multiple cargo types and configurations, with individual graphic panels showing antibody cargo, enzyme cargo, nucleic acid cargo, and versatile configurations;

Slide 8 - Alector Brain Carrier Tool compound demonstrates deep brain penetration - enables brain tissue concentration of ~18nM in NHP and compares favorably with other blood brain barrier technologies;

Slide 9 - Alector ABC Shows Superior Serum Half-Life, Safety, and Brain Penetration in Rodents.

Pipeline focuses on Alzheimer's disease with diverse programs for other brain diseases.

Overview

Presentation slide 4 below sets out Alector's pipeline:

Lead program

Latozinemab (AL001) in potential treatment of frontotemporal dementia with a granulin gene mutation (FTD-GRN) is Alector's lead program. The panel below from Presentation Slide 5 provides a nice rundown of its development status:

The 10-K advises:

... FTD is a severe, rapidly progressing neurodegenerative disorder that affects 50,000 to 60,000 people in the United States and approximately 110,000 people in the European Union. FTD has several heritable forms, with FTD-GRN accounting for an estimated 5% to 10% of all individuals living with the condition. ...

Taking the midpoints of 7.5% of the 55,000 patients thought to have FTD in the US, that tallies 4,125 patients with FTD-GRN. This is an ample size for an orphan drug; however, it is not one that would be forgiving of too much competitive pressure.

The 10-K alerts to the deep competition developing for various products addressing neurological diseases that cross the blood-brain barrier. However, in terms of therapies specifically directed at FTD-GRN it only mentions two:

... Prevail Therapeutics, Inc. and Passage Bio, Inc. are developing gene therapy based products (PR006 and PBFT02, respectively) for the treatment of FTD-GRN.

Recourse to a search query for FTD-GRN on clinicaltrials.gov returns 13 entries. Two are Alector trials; Passage Bio's is a phase 1/2 study with an estimated primary completion date of 08/2028. Prevail's is a Phase 1/2 with an 04/2030 primary completion date.

Excepting Alector, none has advanced beyond phase 1/2. Denali Therapeutics Inc. (DNLI) is a phase 1/2 with an expected completion date of 11/2025, depending on the data from this trial it could develop into a competitive situation sooner than either Passage or Prevail.

Second lead program

AL101/GSK4527226 in potential treatment of Alzheimer's disease [AD] is Alector's second lead therapy. As is the case with latozinemab, it is designed to elevate PGRN levels in the brain, but as stated in 10-K:

... distinct pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties that may be more suitable for treating prevalent neurodegenerative diseases.

AD is so insidiously prevalent in the US that there are concerns that new treatments could impact Medicare's financial viability. This concern came to a head with the FDA's 06/07/2021 approval of Biogen's (BIIB) ADUHELM (aducanumab). Biogen subsequently removed ADUHELM from the market.

There have been subsequent approvals, including:

  1. Leqembi (lecanemab-irmb) - approved in 2023 to treat adult patients with AD, by reducing amyloid plaques that form in the brain, a defining pathophysiological feature of the disease;
  2. Kisunla (donanemab-azbt) - approved in 2024 to treat adult patients with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia stage of disease;
  3. Rexulti (brexpiprazole) - approved in 2023 to treat agitation associated with dementia due to AD.

The bottom line is that AD is a big tent disease with room for several different approaches to treatment. It has proved a tough nut to crack, most recently when Alector's AL002 slid off its pipeline following its failure:

...to meet the primary endpoint of slowing of Alzheimer's clinical progression as measured by the Clinical Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes (CDR®-SB), and there were no treatment effects that favored AL002 on secondary clinical and functional endpoints. Similarly, there were no significant effects on Alzheimer's fluid biomarkers favoring AL002, and amyloid PET imaging demonstrated no treatment-related reduction of brain amyloid levels.

GSK collaboration

As stated in the 10-K:

In July 2021, we entered into a Collaboration and License Agreement with Glaxo Wellcome UK Limited, a subsidiary of GlaxoSmithKline plc ..., to collaborate on the global development and commercialization of progranulin (PGRN)-elevating monoclonal antibodies, including latozinemab and AL101/GSK4527226.

Alector has already received $700 million under the deal with $1.5 billion in additional potential milestones. The deal was amended in 05/2023 to require Alector to pay up to $140.5 million for the conduct of the initial Phase 2 trial for AL101 in Alzheimer's disease.

The deal calls for the equal sharing of profits and losses from US commercialization of latozinemab and AL101. Ex-US commercialization requires GSK to pay double digit tiered royalties to Alector.

Other pipeline molecules

As shown in its pipeline slide above, Alector has:

  1. two additional molecules in its wholly-owned proprietary preclinical pipeline; and
  2. three additional molecules in its wholly-owned proprietary research pipeline.

Each of these five molecules is being evaluated for its potential as an AD treatment, with the exception of GCase-ERT which is being evaluated as a treatment for Parkinson's, Gaucher disease, and Lewy body dementia.

Alector's liquidity runs to H2/2027?

Slide 4 above boasts of liquidity of $354.5 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of 03/31/2025, providing a cash runway into H2/2027.

Its annual cash burn as reflected by cash used in operating activities as stated in its 10-K is ~$184 for 2023 and ~$230 million for 2024. For Q1/ 2025 it is ~$61 million substantially unchanged from Q1/2024.

When I check the sufficiency of its $354.5 liquidity as of 03/31/2025 to take it to H2 2027, it comes up short. There are 2.25 years from 03/31/2025 to H2/2027; $354.5 million divided by 2.25 is ~$157.3 million. This $157.3 million is well short of its annual cash burn.

This suggests that Alector is counting on some unspecified contribution from its collaboration partner in order for its liquidity to extend to H2/2025.

Expectations have been running hot for Alector.

The graph below shows that Wall Street Analysts have been revising their expectations for Alector with four upward revisions and none down:

Over recent quarters, Alector has consistently beaten analyst consensus expectations by losing less than expected, as shown by the chart below:

Conclusion

Alector has one near term prospect with significant potential in its lead pipeline molecule, Latozinemab. With such a low market cap this might be an interesting speculation. I am passing on it. Its recent AD failure tarnishes its prospects, in my view.

Additionally, if it scores approval of Latozinemab, it has to share with GSK. GSK has vastly superior experience and financial resources to those of Alector. Its goals will likely prevail over Alector's. Accordingly, I rate Alector a "Hold".

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Amazing Financials And Ready For A Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) is currently the number one US fresh egg producer, with 43.8 million laying hens and 1.3 billion dozen eggs sold annually as of FY2025. The company is currently in an amazing financial position and is looking into ways to diversify its portfolio and rely less on the volatility of eggs. However, as the stock is following egg prices and is up about 48% since a year ago on the back of eggs breaking record levels in recent history, a few months ago, let’s see how sustainable this valuation is.

We see an overall stable production in recent years as the company adapted to the environment and slowly expanded itself into the largest fresh egg producer in the US. The company has a market share of roughly 24%, based on its latest estimates.

They have a diverse set of clients, including Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Food Lion (OTCQX:ADRNY), which also ties them to the economy. Still, eggs are an essential, high-protein food that remains affordable throughout the cycle and versatile, often being backed by solid demand despite the prices.

The stock has been quite volatile in the years until 2024, when the company basically doubled on account of significant volatility in the egg market, following the price of eggs quite closely. Still, CALM has a history of solid performance throughout the cycle, but the company’s actions may help solidify this into a less cycle-dependent business, as I’ll mention below. This is the result of their investments and acquisitions, although for now, egg prices will remain the key factor in determining their performance.

We can see a very significant jump in cash flows back in 2023 and especially in the past fiscal year, backed by a major jump in the price of eggs. Like any other commodity company, their numbers improved significantly during the upside, and we recently saw the stock rally after the higher prices boosted their latest earnings. I’ll talk more about this below, but keep in mind that this cycle is normal for commodities, including eggs. To get an idea, the average selling price for conventional eggs in the latest quarter was $3.78 per dozen vs. $2.06 a year ago, so nearly a 2x in price, for reasons I’ll also mention below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that Cal-Maine is in an excellent financial position, with the current assets covering the TOTAL liabilities nearly 4 times! Their $499.4 million in cash is almost enough to cover the total liabilities, and the company has zero debt, meaning they should prove to be very resilient and won’t have to worry about the price of eggs even during the downside, being a financial fortress that can withstand many years of losses before it would feel a significant negative effect (it can take several years of downcycle to burn through all their cash/securities). Still, there’s $2.56 billion in equity on a ~$5.13 billion market cap, placing the PB at ~2.

The company currently has $1.39 billion in net cash, but an important thing to mention here is that this wasn’t always the case. Yes, the company was always in a great position with net cash, but back in 2022, for example, they only had $174.5 million in net cash, which is a very important part of the valuation. It will be very tough to see them lose this amazing financial position, and at “worst”, I’d expect them to do significant acquisitions, which should be accretive for the cash flow, so it's still good for them in the long term regardless.

Dividends & Buybacks

The dividend currently stands at a very significant yield since the company is rewarding investors following the boom in egg prices that resulted in record levels of cash flows and net income. However, as prices revert to their mean, we should not expect this type of dividend forever. As seen several times in the past 10 years, the company can even pay zero dividends, so don’t expect a stable dividend from a commodity producer.

They allocate a third of the net income under their variable dividend policy, which is why the last few dividends have been so significant. It might seem like much, but the past 5 years would represent an average of ~3.13% from the current price, and that’s despite 2 of their highest dividends ever. This is why buying any commodity company during high prices is generally bad—with few exceptions—as even the great times would come with limited results compared to buying during the downside, which can result in significant double-digit yields during the upside, on top of potential multi-baggers.

Risks & Opportunities

For now, keep in mind that the company will depend on egg prices. They saw nice numbers over the past years, backed by significantly above-average egg prices, but we will most likely see them revert over the following years.

Thanks to the higher egg prices, the company saw an amazing FY2023 and especially FY2025, despite it reverting and the cash flow then falling by ~50% in 2024. They are using these cash flows to expand into less volatile sectors, but the company’s earnings will likely continue to depend significantly on egg prices in the near future. Still, we see several acquisitions made by Cal-Maine over the past years, such as probably their most ambitious investment yet (Echo Lake Foods earlier this year) for $258 million, expanding their offering in the ready-to-eat egg products and breakfast foods market (i.e., waffles, pancakes, pre-cooked omelets).

This type of acquisition can help stabilize the company’s revenue to some degree, as food prices will not present the same volatility as an individual commodity, while the company is able to integrate this into its model and likely deliver significant synergies since it's supplying its own eggs. This comes on top of their 51% stake in Crepini, a producer of similar egg-based prepared foods, as well as the expansion of their MeadowCreek Foods, a hard-boiled egg producer. Overall, we can see them vertically integrating into more of a food company rather than just a commodity producer, which could provide more stability and predictability, which the market should appreciate and even trade at better multiples, especially if they continue this trend.

As mentioned above, the price of eggs has nearly doubled since a year ago, and the company provided a very clear reason as to why:

The higher market prices were a direct result of the reduced supply of shell eggs across the industry due to outbreaks of highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) during a period of high demand for eggs and egg products around the Easter holiday.

The USDA announced up to $1 billion worth of investments in the industry to help fight HPAI, so we are likely to see this normalize eventually. So, we’re looking at a commodity whose price has been boosted by a mix of natural/environmental causes at a time when egg demand was at its highest—Easter. In a way, this was a “perfect storm” for Cal-Maine, and it’s not impossible to see this happen in the future as well. Having worked with specialists in the industry some years ago, I’ve heard stories of how even the most careful farms that paid very significant attention to healthcare in an effort of fighting some disease that was common at the time failed, with a single mistake leading to the farm being forced to wipe out the entire herd, resulting in very significant losses. This is unfortunately common for the industry, and we are likely to see this happen from time to time, adding to the cyclicality of the prices. High prices will then attract more production, which should be visible relatively quickly in the market since this industry, unlike mining, has much faster capital turnover than mining, for example, which requires even a decade from exploration to permitting, financing, developing, and mining.

The stock is up nearly 60% over the past year, backed by the higher price of eggs and the company’s results, but we also have to note the significant ~11.23% short interest. This means that there is some potential for a short squeeze, which recently made the stock jump on the back of the WallStreetBets hype, together with many other highly-shorted stocks. This short interest likely comes as a result of the higher prices, which are expected to be only temporary due to the reasons mentioned above, which won’t last forever. As egg prices normalize, the stock should follow to some degree, especially in the short and medium-term, until their investments lead to a weaker connection between the company and egg prices. This could also lead to better multiples, as they could shift from a pure-play commodity company to a mixture of commodities and food, the latter potentially adding more stability.

Peer Comparison

For a peer comparison, I’ll go with a mixture of direct competitors such as Vital Farms (VITL), but also other companies in the packaged foods and meats market, such as Post Holdings (POST), Tyson Foods (TSN), J.M. Smucker (SJM), Hormel Foods (HRL), and Conagra Brands (CAG), to get a broader idea about the industry, including prepared meals and other food products.

Compared to Vital Farms, we see that Cal-Maine is significantly undervalued if we look at any valuation metrics, including the PB (which doesn’t have much to do with egg prices). Still, even compared to the broader industry, CALM seems to be cheaper than all the other companies. However, what I wanted to point out with this comparison is the kind of ratios we can expect. Right now, CALM is so low, especially because of the very high egg prices. Have these numbers fall in half when egg prices normalize, and we are suddenly at similar numbers to these peers—nothing exceptional. So, in reality, CALM is not really undervalued if we adjust for a normal/average price throughout the cycle.

Valuation

As for a DCF valuation of Cal-Maine, I believe $175 million should be a fair average FCF to expect throughout a normal cycle, especially backed by their investments and inflation, assuming they cut the CAPEX during bad times from the recent all-time highs we’ve seen lately. This FCF is still projected to grow at a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years and then at a 3% CAGR for the other 5 years (in line with long-term egg demand expectations). With a 2% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 6.5%—in line with market estimates—and considering a very significant $1.39 billion in net cash along with 48.5 million shares outstanding, the resulting DCF model yields an enterprise value of approximately $4.33 billion. After adding the net cash, this leads to an equity value of roughly $5.72 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $117.86.

That’s slightly above the current price, and although it may seem like much given the past stock performance, I’ll mention again that their financials changed significantly. Their significant net cash position will be hard to burn through, and if the management plays it right, it can turn out to be very accretive for their free cash flows, potentially opening the way for significant expansion—in or outside the US, vertically or horizontally.

Conclusions

Overall, Cal-Maine seems to be in a position where it can really transform the business from a volatile commodity producer into one with a more stable business model, which could potentially attract and support a higher valuation for longer. Their excellent financial position and net cash represent a big advantage, allowing the management to reshape the company for a better future. I rate Cal-Maine a Buy now for their long-term potential, but keep in mind that we are talking about relying quite a bit on the management to make the right moves in a volatile environment, so don’t think this is a risk-free investment.

data.title: Civeo: Management's Transformative Acquisition Makes Shares Even Cheaper
data.description:
In my opinion, one of the most interesting companies on the market has got to be Civeo Corporation (NYSE:CVEO). This is because it is such a unique firm. For those not familiar with it, it essentially provides accommodation services for the natural resource extraction industries in Canada and Australia. It provides temporary housing for those working in remote areas, as well as services associated with that housing. Financial performance recently, and in fact for the last few years now, has been a bit disappointing. Even so, shares of the business trade at rock bottom levels. This was why, in February of this year, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. Since then, shares have risen by 7.5%, outpacing the 5.6% increase achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Honestly, I believe that continued outperformance is justified. This is especially true after management made a big acquisition and landed a large contract that should create additional revenue and cash flows moving forward. I don't like the fact that this required a pretty sizable increase in net debt after management had worked so hard to get that down. But in the long run, I think it will all be worth it. Of course, as new data comes out, investors would be wise to incorporate that into the picture. And it just so happens that, before the market opens on July 29th, management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, they expect continued weakness. But that's not enough to change my overall assessment at this time.

A look at recent results

Fundamentally speaking, things have not been particularly great for Civeo Corporation. To understand what I mean, we need only look at the results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year compared to the same time last year. During that time, revenue for the company contracted from $166.1 million to $144 million. To understand this, it would be best to dig into each of its operating segments.

The problem child for the company ended up being its Canadian segment. According to management, revenues associated with that part of the company fell sharply from $67.2 million to $40.4 million. This came as the number of billed rooms plummeted from 610,032 to 358,697. Management blamed this on the fact that oil sands operators have decided to prioritize reducing operating costs as opposed to maximizing output. The firm saw reduced occupancy at its Sitka Lodge as well as the Kitimat LNG facility, which is located nearest to completion. And there ended up being reduced mobile asset activity from pipeline projects that were completed early last year. It is worth mentioning that the average daily rate for lodges also dropped, declining from $98 to $93. This is a reflection of overall market weakness. Honestly, I don't find this to be surprising in the least bit. WCS crude in the first quarter of 2025, for instance, came in at $58.27 per barrel. Although that is only marginally lower than the $59.48 per barrel reported the previous year, over the same window of time, WTI crude prices dropped 7.3% in light of broader economic concerns. Overall weakness in the energy industry is bound to have a deleterious effect on companies such as this.

As painful as operations in Canada ended up being, the exact opposite was true in Australia. Revenue there actually jumped from $91.7 million to $103.6 million. This was in spite of the fact that the average daily rate for villages contracted from $77 to $75. Meanwhile, the firm saw a rise in the number of billed rooms from 613,936 to 625,636. Even with this improvement, however, total accommodation revenue contracted. The real driver here was on the food service and other services revenue side of things, which skyrocketed from $44.6 million to $56.8 million. This is because of new business in its integrated services villages in Western Australia. This stemmed from a six-year contract that the company won that should bring with it around A$1.4 billion in revenue during its lifetime.

The drop in revenue for the company brought with it a decline in profitability, also. Net profits went from negative $5.1 million to negative $9.8 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company booked a $7.8 million impairment charge in the first quarter of last year and was driven in part by a $6.1 million gain on the sale of its McClelland Lake Lodge assets. Certain expenses, such as selling, general, and administrative costs, ended up being sticky year over year. The same applied to depreciation and amortization costs. Other profitability metrics also worsened. Operating cash flow went from $6 million to negative $8.4 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $12.3 million to $6.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company pulled back from $17.8 million to $12.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2024 fiscal year compared to 2023. This shows continued weakness, driven almost entirely by the firm's Canadian operations. In the image below, you can see a breakdown of key metrics that shows a reduction in activity in Canada, even as operations in Australia picked up. Naturally, this had a negative impact on the bottom line for the company, also, with profits and cash flows dropping year over year.

For the current fiscal year in its entirety, management has provided mixed guidance. Overall revenue is now expected to be between $620 million and $650 million. Even at the high end, that would represent another year-over-year decline. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company is anticipated to be between $75 million and $85 million. The midpoint there would almost exactly match what the company saw last year. So at least in that regard, things are looking more solid.

It is vital that we consider the fact that this guidance does not factor in the four villages that management announced the acquisition of in February of this year. These are based in the Australian Bowen Basin and will cost shareholders a whopping $67 million. As the chart below illustrates, this will increase the net debt picture of the company rather significantly. The good news, however, is that the transaction does make shares cheaper. This is because the overall annual revenue associated with this business is estimated to be around $32 million. From that, management expects to extract EBITDA of approximately $17 million a year.

This purchase price implies an EV to EBITDA multiple of about 3.9. This is lower than the 5.6 multiple that Civeo Corporation is trading at without factoring in the acquisition. Management does have an Australian revolving credit facility that has an annualized interest rate of about 6.8%. Using the assumption that management will utilize that for the purchase, it is possible to not only look at the pro forma EBITDA for the company but also to look at the pro forma operating cash flow.

Using these assumptions, I was able to show in the chart above how shares of the company are priced using pro forma figures for 2024 and pro forma estimates for 2025, and also to look at how this stacks up against the company under the assumption that the transaction would never be completed. As you can see, the stock is certainly cheaper as a result of this purchase. But the downside to this is that overall net leverage is expected to rise. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. This is naturally unfortunate to see. But considering the lower trading multiple, I don't have much of an issue.

There are some other reasons to be optimistic. In May of this year, management announced that it was awarded a three-year contract with a major metallurgical coal producer in Australia to provide integrated services at two villages in the Bowen Basin. This arrangement, which will be completed over three years, should generate around $40.1 million in revenue for the business. However, this was factored into the recent guidance provided by management. The firm also received, in June of this year, a four-year contract renewal to provide rooms and hospitality services in that same area in a deal valued at $159.5 million over the span of four years. Although this was also incorporated into the guidance for the year, it is a sign that the future for the company should pick up.

It will be interesting to see the extent to which these contracts could affect backlog moving forward. In the chart above, you can see that, at the end of the most recent quarter, Civeo Corporation had a backlog of $519.1 million. That is down from the $564.8 million it had at the end of last year. Overall, the backlog has been trending lower. But acquisitions and big contract wins are guaranteed to affect it in a positive light.

This is just one thing that investors should be paying attention to when management announces financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year before the market opens on July 29th. As the table below illustrates, analysts believe that revenue will contract from $188.7 million last year to $163 million this year. Earnings per share and overall net profits are expected to worsen, also. The table also shows cash flow figures for the second quarter of 2024. It will be interesting to see whether these worsen as well. Even so, as long as management doesn't materially alter guidance that has been put forward already, I don't see a reason to panic.

Takeaway

Although the last few years have been a difficult time for shareholders of Civeo Corporation, I believe that the company remains attractively priced. Recent moves made by management have increased the risk profile because of the rise in leverage that they necessitate. But at the same time, the major acquisition made by the firm reduces the overall trading multiple of the company and should boost financial results in the long run. Due to these factors, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

data.title: Whitecap Resources: First Post-Merger Results & 2030 Projection Update
data.description:
In my previous Whitecap (TSX:WCP:CA) and (OTCPK:WCPRF) articles, I explained why I sold all my other oil producers and placed a large bet on the Veren-Whitecap merger. First, the dividend yield exceeded 9%, with room for further growth. Second, the downside was well protected.

The recent Q2 results show that everything is progressing as planned, with results strongly beating analysts' estimates. My projection model continues to show a double-digit yield, so I continue holding a 12% core portfolio position. That said, if the stock price were to rise another 20-30% with no improvement in oil/gas prices, I would consider trimming the position to ~8%.

Q2 Merger and Early Beat

Whitecap has officially merged with Veren as of May 12th, so the second quarter's production only reflects Veren's production from that date onward. This resulted in 292.8 kboe/d, with EPS of $0.33, beating estimates by $0.16.

What interests me more than short-term EPS results is the development of the announced C$200 million in annual savings from synergies. During the call, CFO Thanh Kang commented that he estimates C$35 million of synergies have already been achieved due to improvements in credit scoring and reductions in staff.

The merger is huge for the company as it is approximately doubling its scale. It's still too early for a detailed and reliable estimation, so we will have to wait for the numbers by early November, when the management plans to release the 2026 budget.

Since Veren's production numbers have been included since mid-May, Whitecap estimates achieving ~297,500 boe/d of production in 2025.

With a current production pace of 365,000 boe/d, $65 WTI, and C$3 AECO, management expects to generate around C$1.4 billion of free cash flow. The $900 million cost of the base dividend (6.8% yield) means management will have an additional $500 million to allocate between growth and buybacks.

What do I model going forward?

Despite having a sound macro-thesis about oil and gas, I believe that the commodity prices are nearly unpredictable; thus, I always think in scenarios.

Base Case

When I invest, my base case always considers the prices of commodity futures. In the end, the company can always hedge those prices, or so can you. It means that for the base case projection, I am using prices WTI $65, HH $4, and AECO C$3.

For the costs, royalties, and taxes, I am using guidance from page 25 of the presentation. Furthermore, I project C$35 million in synergy savings for 2025 and the full C$200 million in savings for 2026 onward.

We will have to wait for the November update about the growth plan, but for now, I model a C$500-C$700 million growth CapEx above the C$2 billion maintenance CapEx for 2026-2028. After that, I assume no growth capital, which results in an approximately 15% FCF yield that management can decide how to allocate.

For a closer look at the numbers, here is a graph of my projection for 2026 with those assumed commodity prices and production of 378 kboe/d.

Bull Case

Compared to the base case, I'm just adjusting the WTI to $80. Under such pricing, the company yield would quickly rise towards 20%, which would likely result in a doubling of the share price. The company would likely spend more on growth CapEx, as it has many options for growth post-merger.

The bull case could be further enhanced by higher-than-expected synergies. Management explained that the C$200 million is a base case, and they believe there will be more savings realized over time.

One more area that requires improvement and could potentially boost free cash flow is their natural gas marketing. For now, most of their gas is sold for AECO prices. The company targets 20% exposure to LNG pricing. There is no contract on the table at present, so it is not included in my projection. However, 20% of their gas linked to JKM pricing could generate an additional C$200-250 million in free cash flow. Additionally, a C$1 improvement in AECO yields an additional C$200 million in FCF.

Bear Case

My last article was pretty much all about the bear case. I took the company through the pricing from COVID lows, naked, without any hedging. The survival without the need for a dividend cut made me buy the large position. As of now, the company is hedging 22% of its oil production and 29% of its gas production for 2026, with debt down to C$3.3 billion, or 1x Net Debt/Funds Flow, due to non-core asset sales during the quarter.

Conclusion

Following the successful second quarter, the market now has greater confidence in the merger's execution. The share price has recovered sharply from the recent lows. That said, my model still yields a double-digit FCF under modest assumptions, so I am still holding Whitecap as my only oil holding.

If the price were to rise another 20–30% without an improvement in commodity prices, I would see the shares as fairly valued and would consider moving part of my oil allocation towards other oil names, such as Canadian Natural (CNQ), which has recently underperformed.

If you found this analysis useful, don’t forget to comment and follow for quarterly updates, deep scenario modeling, and risk-first positioning.
